1: A theme: the missional call — "make his deeds known among the nations" — is the OT anticipation of the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19-20; Acts 1:8). What the psalm calls Israel to do for YHWH, the NT says Christ accomplishes through his followers. The nations are the audience not of religious information but of the proclaimed deeds of God in history, now centered on the Christ-event.
2: A theme: the praise of God expressed in singing is taken up in Christ, who leads his brothers in worship. Hebrews 2:12 applies Psalms 22:22 to Christ: "I will declare your name to my brothers; in the midst of the assembly I will sing your praise." The song that the psalm calls Israel to sing is the song Christ himself sings as the firstborn among many brothers (Romans 8:29).
3: A theme: "boast in his holy name" — Philippians 2:9-11 names the name above every name as the name of Jesus; Galatians 6:14 says "may I never boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ." The holy name in which Israel boasts is ultimately the name of the Son, who perfectly bears and expresses the character of the Father (John 1:18; Psalms 17:6).
4: A theme: "seek the LORD and his power; seek his presence at all times" — in Christ this seeking is answered definitively. John 14:6 ("I am the way and the truth and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me") and John 1:18 present Christ as the presence and power of God made accessible. To seek God's face is now to encounter the face of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
5: A revelation of God: YHWH is the God who performs signs, wonders, and judgments — his character disclosed through mighty acts. Jesus's signs in John's Gospel stand in this tradition: John 6:14 identifies him as "the Prophet who is to come" after the feeding miracle; the signs reveal that the God of the Exodus is present and acting in Christ. "Wonderful deeds" (niplāʾôt) frames the signs as theological disclosure, not merely miracle.
6: A theme: "children of Abraham, his servant" — Galatians 3:29 redefines this identity in Christ: "if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to the promise." The covenant lineage is not cancelled but fulfilled — the "children of Abraham" now includes all who share Abraham's faith, united in Christ the true seed (Galatians 3:16: "the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed... meaning one person, who is Christ").
7: A revelation of God: YHWH is universal judge — "his judgments are in all the earth." The NT presents Christ as the appointed agent of this universal judgment: "the Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son" (John 5:22); "he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed" (Acts 17:31). Universal sovereignty is already implicit in the psalm and made explicit in Christ.
8: A direct connection: "he keeps his covenant in memory forever" — Luke 1:54-55 and Psalms 1:72-73 say God "remembered his covenant" in the Incarnation. The canticles of Luke deliberately invoke this theological tradition: YHWH's covenant memory, kept "forever," is activated in Christ. The new covenant (Luke 22:20 — "this cup is the new covenant in my blood") is the ultimate expression of covenant-memory fulfilled in blood.
9: A type: "the covenant he confirmed to Abraham, and his sworn promise to Isaac" — Paul calls this the proto-Gospel in Galatians 3:8 ("the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith and announced the gospel in advance to Abraham"). Galatians 3:15-17 argues that Christ is the mediator of this covenant; what was made with Abraham was always pointing to Christ as the singular heir through whom the blessing flows to all nations.
10: A theme: "he confirmed it to Jacob as a decree, to Israel as an everlasting statute" — Christ's statement "I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it" (Matthew 5:17) answers this everlasting statute. The covenant established with the patriarchs is not voided but brought to its intended goal (telos) in Christ (Romans 10:4). The statute is everlasting because the one who fulfills it is eternal.
11: A shadow: "to you I will give the land of Canaan as your inheritance" — the land promise is the first layer of a theological theme that expands in the NT. Hebrews 11:16 says the patriarchs were looking for "a better country — a heavenly one"; Matthew 5:5 reinterprets "the meek shall inherit the land" as inheriting the earth; Revelation 21:1-4 presents the renewed creation as the fulfillment. What Israel received as a down payment in Canaan, the redeemed receive in full through the Heir of all things (Hebrews 1:2).
12: A revelation of God: God works through the small and vulnerable to accomplish his purposes. The incarnation of Christ into a marginal people under Roman occupation, born in a stable with no room at the inn (Luke 2:7), continues the pattern. Divine election runs counter to human expectations of power and size: "God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong" (1 Corinthians 1:27).
13: A revelation of God: "they wandered from nation to nation, from one kingdom to another people" — the patriarchal sojourn as the condition of covenant belonging. Hebrews 11:13-16 calls them "strangers and exiles on the earth." Christ himself was a refugee in Egypt (Matthew 2:13-15) and had "nowhere to lay his head" (Luke 9:58), sharing the sojourner-condition of the covenant people and validating it as the shape of life under the cross.
14: A revelation of God: "he allowed no one to oppress them; he rebuked kings on their behalf" — divine protection of the covenant line through which Christ would come. The same providential protection guards the line of promise from the patriarchs to the flight into Egypt (Matthew 2:13-15). God guards the line of promise because that line ultimately produces the Messiah.
15: A direct connection: "do not touch my anointed ones; do not harm my prophets" — meshîḥîm (anointed ones) is the plural of meshîḥ (Messiah/Christ). The term here protects the patriarchs but points forward to the Anointed One par excellence. The protected prophets form the chain of testimony culminating in Christ — who alone is ultimately "harmed" precisely so that harm falls on no one else.
16: A type: "when he called down famine over the land" — divine sovereign ordering of events to bring Joseph to prominence in Egypt. This launches the Joseph narrative, the most extended OT type of Christ. The famine that creates the crisis God uses to exalt his chosen servant anticipates the circumstances through which Christ's path to glory runs: through the cross to the crown (Philippians 2:8-9).
17: A type: "he sent a man ahead of them — Joseph, sold as a slave" — Acts 7:9 treats Joseph as a direct antecedent of Jesus, both handed over by those who should have received them. Sold for twenty pieces of silver by his brothers (Genesis 37:28) / Jesus sold for thirty by his disciple (Matthew 26:15; cf. Zechariah 11:12-13). The "sending ahead" — prior to the salvation he would accomplish — types Christ sent as the forerunner who prepares the way (John 14:2-3; Hebrews 6:20).
18: A type: "they bruised his feet with shackles, his neck was put in an iron collar" — Joseph's imprisonment and physical suffering precede his exaltation. Philippians 2:8 follows the same pattern: "he humbled himself and became obedient to death." The iron collar anticipates the crown of thorns; the shackles anticipate the nails. Humiliation preceding exaltation is the irreducible pattern of both Joseph and Christ.
19: A type: "this lasted until what he had foretold came true — all the while the word of the LORD was testing him" — the divine word that predicted Joseph's dreams tested him by delaying fulfillment. In Christ, "when the fullness of time had come" (Galatians 4:4) — God acted according to a precise divine timetable. Christ's suffering was the test the word of God worked through before the promise was fulfilled (Hebrews 5:8: "he learned obedience from what he suffered").
20: A type: "the king sent word and freed him; the ruler of the peoples set him at liberty" — Joseph's release from imprisonment to royal authority is one of the OT's most direct resurrection types. Acts 2:24 uses the same logic for Christ: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." The imprisoned servant released by divine intervention to rule is the pattern both embody.
21: A type: "he made him master of his household, ruler of all he possessed" — Joseph's exaltation to Pharaoh's right hand (Genesis 41:40-43) types Christ's session at the Father's right hand. Psalms 110:1 ("sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool") is the explicit messianic application; Ephesians 1:20-22 applies this to Christ: God "seated him at his right hand... and placed all things under his feet." The Joseph-figure exalted to the ruler's side is the OT's structural preview.
22: A type: "to instruct his princes at his pleasure and teach his elders wisdom" — Joseph's authority to instruct the powerful in Egypt anticipates Christ's authority as teacher. Matthew 7:29 says Jesus taught "as one who had authority, not as their teachers of the law"; 1 Corinthians 1:30 calls Christ "our wisdom from God." The exalted servant who teaches the powerful shadows the exalted Christ who is wisdom incarnate.
23: A type: "Israel entered Egypt, Jacob sojourned in Ham's territory" — Matthew 2:14-15 cites this pattern directly, applying Hosea 11:1 ("out of Egypt I called my son") to Jesus's sojourn in Egypt. The evangelist reads the Exodus typologically: Israel-in-Egypt types Christ-in-Egypt; the calling "out of Egypt" fulfilled first in the Exodus is the pattern re-enacted in Christ's return. The descent into Egypt is a type of the incarnation — the Son entering foreign territory before being called home.
24: A revelation of God: "the LORD made his people very fruitful" — multiplication of the covenant people despite opposition is YHWH's pattern. John 15:5 ("those who remain in me... will bear much fruit") applies the growth-pattern to Christ and his people. The fruitfulness of Israel in Egypt types the fruitfulness of the Church in an opposed world, both dependent on divine blessing, not human strategy.
25: A revelation of God: "he turned their hearts to hate his people" — divine sovereignty ordering even the hostility of Pharaoh. Acts 4:27-28 uses the same logic for the crucifixion: Herod and Pilate, Gentiles and Israel "did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." Even Christ's enemies serve the purposes of the one who "turned their hearts," making the cross the supreme example of God's sovereignty over hostile human will.
26: A type: "he sent Moses his servant, and Aaron whom he had chosen" — Moses the servant-deliverer prefigures Christ in Deuteronomy 18:15 ("a prophet like me"), applied to Jesus in Acts 3:22. Aaron prefigures the high priest: Hebrews 5:4 says Aaron was called by God, not self-appointed; Christ likewise (Hebrews 5:5-6). Both prophet and high priest are united in Christ, who is simultaneously the prophet who speaks God's word and the priest who mediates through sacrifice.
27: A type: "they performed his signs among them, worked wonders in Egypt" — the signs of Moses and Aaron type Jesus's signs. John 6:14 after the feeding miracle: "Surely this is the Prophet who is to come" — the crowd reads the sign against the background of Moses's wonder-working. Where the Mosaic signs pointed to Sinai, the Johannine signs point to the cross and resurrection as the definitive act of divine self-disclosure.
28: A shadow: "he sent darkness and made the land dark" — the ninth plague (Exodus 10:21-23) anticipates the darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45: "from the sixth hour to the ninth hour darkness came over all the land"). Both are signs of divine judgment; the darkness at Calvary signals that Christ is absorbing the judgment that the Egyptian plagues historically embodied — the plague falls on the Deliverer, not his people.
29: A shadow: the plagues are instruments of covenant judgment. Galatians 3:13 ("Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us") is the NT logic: the curse-judgments that fell on Egypt in the Exodus are previews of the judgment Christ absorbs at Calvary. His people are delivered through his blood — as Israel was, through the Passover blood on the doorposts — not by the blood of their enemies.
30: A shadow: "their land swarmed with frogs, even in the chambers of their kings" — the plague that invaded the centers of worldly power. In Christ the Gospel itself penetrates "even in Caesar's household" (Philippians 4:22). The divine advance against human power-structures previewed in the Exodus plagues continues in the spread of Christ's kingdom — no human power, however entrenched, is beyond the reach of God's intervention.
31: A revelation of God: "he spoke and there came swarms of flies, gnats throughout their territory" — God accomplishes judgment through his word alone. "He spoke and it came to be" (Psalms 33:9). Christ is the Word through whom all things were made and through whom all divine purposes are accomplished (John 1:3; Hebrews 1:3 — "sustaining all things by his powerful word").
32: A shadow: "he gave them hail for rain, lightning through their land" — storm as divine judgment. The seventh plague (Exodus 9:23-26) becomes eschatological judgment imagery in Revelation 8:7; Psalms 11:19; Psalms 16:21. Christ's stilling of the storm (Mark 4:39) shows his authority over the elements that bring judgment — he is master of the powers that embody divine wrath, pointing to his authority to face judgment at Calvary and emerge victorious.
33: A shadow: "he struck their vines and fig trees, shattered the trees" — the fig tree is a prophetic symbol for Israel (Jeremiah 8:13; Hosea 9:10; Joel 1:7). Christ's cursing of the barren fig tree (Mark 11:12-14, 20-21) is a prophetic sign-act echoing the plague-judgments: unfruitful religious structures under divine judgment. The destroyed trees of the plague become the withered fig tree of the Passion week.
34: A shadow: "he spoke and the locusts came, grasshoppers without number" — the locust plague (Exodus 10:4-19) is the background for Revelation 9:3 and the eschatological locust-judgment of Joel 1-2. Christ who averts the plague for those under his blood (1 Corinthians 5:7 — "our Passover lamb") brings the full harvest-judgment on those who are not. The plague-averted becomes the eschatological judgment only the Lamb can prevent.
35: A shadow: "they consumed every plant in their land" — total destruction as covenantal judgment. Hebrews 12:29 ("our God is a consuming fire") stands in this tradition. Christ who is the vine (John 15:1) warns that fruitless branches are "thrown into the fire and burned" (John 15:6). The consuming judgment of the Exodus plagues reaches its final form in Christ's eschatological judgment at the harvest.
36: A type: "he struck down every firstborn in their land" — the Passover judgment. 1 Corinthians 5:7 makes the typological link explicit: "Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed." The death of Egypt's firstborn is averted for Israel by the lamb's blood; the final judgment is averted for all under the blood of Christ, who is himself "the firstborn from the dead" (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5) — the firstborn who died so that God's firstborn-people might live.
37: A type: "he brought his people out loaded with silver and gold; not one person in all their tribes was weak" — the Exodus plunder and the strength of the redeemed. Colossians 2:15 applies plunder-language to the cross: Christ "disarmed the powers and authorities, making a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them." The Exodus plunder of Egypt types Christ's despoiling of the enemy at Calvary; the redeemed people leave strong because their deliverer has prevailed.
38: A revelation of God: "Egypt was glad when they left, for dread of them had fallen on them" — reversal of the oppressor-and-oppressed dynamic. In Christ's resurrection the ultimate reversal occurs: the powers that crucified him are defeated. Acts 2:36 ("God has made this Jesus both Lord and Christ") declares that the executed one is now the feared one — the full reversal the Exodus only previewed.
39: A type: "he spread out a cloud as a shelter and gave fire to light the way through the night" — the pillar of cloud and fire is the Shekinah, the localized divine presence with Israel. John 1:14 ("the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us — and we have seen his glory") is the explicit fulfillment. Christ is the cloud and fire — the divine presence made incarnate; John 8:12 ("I am the light of the world") applies the fire-pillar image directly to himself.
40: A type: "they asked for food and he brought them quail, satisfying them with bread from heaven" — this text stands directly behind Jesus's feeding miracles and John 6's bread-of-life discourse. John 6:31-35 quotes the manna tradition and Jesus responds: "my Father gives you the true bread from heaven... I am the bread of life." The manna gave temporary physical life; Christ gives life that endures forever (John 6:51). The type is fulfilled in the one who is himself the bread.
41: A type: "he split open the rock and water poured out" — 1 Corinthians 10:4 states the typological connection explicitly: "they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ." The water from the rock (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11) is the NT's most directly named Christ-type. John 7:37-38 applies the same imagery to the Spirit poured out through Christ: "rivers of living water will flow from within them" — water from the rock becomes the Spirit from the crucified side (John 19:34).
42: A direct connection: "for he had called to mind his holy promise to Abraham his servant" — Luke 1:72-73 says God "remembered his holy covenant, the oath he swore to our father Abraham." The Exodus is the first fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise; the Incarnation is the ultimate fulfillment. In Christ, God permanently "remembers" Abraham — the promise is not merely kept but fully redeemed in the one who is Abraham's seed (Galatians 3:16).
43: A type: "he led his people out with rejoicing, his chosen ones with shouts of joy" — the New Exodus motif of Isaiah 51:11; 55:12 is fulfilled in Christ. 1 Peter 1:8 describes the joy of those redeemed by Christ: "you believe in him and are filled with an inexpressible and glorious joy." The joyful departure from bondage types the eschatological rejoicing of the redeemed, who "come to Mount Zion" with "everlasting joy upon their heads" (Isaiah 35:10).
44: A shadow: "he gave them the lands of other nations, and they took possession of what others had worked for" — Israel's inheritance of Canaan types the inheritance of believers in Christ. Romans 8:17 ("co-heirs with Christ"); Hebrews 1:2 ("appointed heir of all things"); Revelation 21:7 ("the one who is victorious will inherit all this") — the land-gift expands to cosmic scope. What Israel received as a down payment in Canaan, the redeemed receive in full through the Heir of all things.
45: A revelation of God: "so that they would keep his decrees and obey his teachings — Praise the LORD!" — the Exodus's stated purpose: covenant-obedience. This goal is fulfilled in Christ through the new covenant: "I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts" (Jeremiah 31:33; Hebrews 8:10-11). What the Exodus could not achieve by law alone — willing, inward obedience — the Spirit of Christ accomplishes (Ezekiel 36:26-27; Romans 8:4). The psalm's closing praise is the response to grace-enabled obedience becoming finally possible.
